title:
Module 2
Base Model Creation
subtitle: Learn How to Generate Your Base Model Realistically and Efficiently
output_filename: Module 2 - Base Model Creation/Module 2 - Base Model Creation.pdf

blocks.content: Before you generate a single image, you need to decide who your AI model is. This is a business decision, not an aesthetic one. Your niche determines your audience, your content style, and ultimately your revenue potential. The biggest mistake beginners make is choosing a niche they personally like instead of one with proven market demand.

blocks.title: Choosing Your Niche — Read This First
blocks.content:
Do not just pick a generic blonde girl — that space is completely oversaturated and you will not stand out. You need to choose a niche that is currently working and has demand. Look at what's trending, what audiences are engaging with, and build your persona around that.

Exotic niches (vitiligo, heterochromia, and similar unique traits) are highly not recommended. Fanvue has been actively cracking down on these types of models. While you can technically sell them on other platforms for AI content, the reality is that generating NSFW content for these models is extremely difficult for no good reason — the tools just don't handle it well. Save yourself the headache and pick a niche that works with the current tools and platforms.

Research what is already working. Look at successful AI influencer pages on Instagram, Threads, and TikTok. Study what aesthetics get engagement, what niches have active audiences, and what styles people actually follow. You are building a product for a market, not creating art for yourself.

items: Age range and look (18-25 is the standard sweet spot for engagement)
Ethnicity and features — pick what resonates with your target audience
Style and aesthetic (e.g., girl-next-door, alt, fitness, elegant, cute)
Personality tone (shy, bold, playful, mysterious) — this shapes all captions
Name — must sound real, be memorable, easy to search, and available across platforms
Girl-next-door / College aesthetic — highest mass appeal, easiest to grow
Fitness / Gym — strong engagement, clear content themes
Alt / Goth — passionate niche audience, less competition
Elegant / Luxury — attracts higher-spending audience
Cute / Soft — strong on Threads and TikTok for discovery

left.title: IMAGE PLACEHOLDER
left.content: Niche selection matrix: 2x2 grid showing Demand (High/Low) vs Competition (High/Low). Best niches labeled in high-demand quadrants. Example niches placed in each quadrant.

items: Pick a niche with proven demand — do not invent a new one
Research what is working on subscription platforms and social media
Your persona must feel like a real person, not a character or cartoon
Avoid overly specific niches that limit content variety
Avoid niches that require complex backgrounds or scenarios to generate
The simpler the niche, the easier it is to maintain consistency

right.content:
Niche: Cute, approachable college-age girl with a soft aesthetic.
Name: Emma, Lily, Sofia — simple, real, memorable.
Why it works: Massive audience, easy to generate content, natural settings.

Niche: 'Cyberpunk alien princess from the future.'
Name: XxDarkAngelxX — unnatural, forgettable.
Why it fails: Tiny audience, hard to generate consistently, looks obviously AI.

items: Sounds like a real person's name — not a brand or character
Easy to spell and remember after seeing it once
Available as a username on Instagram, TikTok, and Threads
Not already used by a well-known creator or public figure
Works with a last initial for username format (e.g., emma.laine, sofiam)

right.title: IMAGE PLACEHOLDER
right.content: Examples of different AI persona styles side by side — show 4 different niche aesthetics with labels: Girl-next-door, Fitness, Alt/Goth, Elegant

blocks.content: Your base model is the core face and body that will remain consistent across all content. Getting this right is the single most important step. If the face changes between images, your entire page looks fake. This lesson covers the exact tools, the two different approaches, and the full process for creating a base model you can reproduce reliably.
The Image Generation Tools

left.content: Nano Banana 2 is your primary tool for all SFW (safe for work) content. This is what you will use for Instagram, Threads, TikTok, and any public-facing content. It produces highly photorealistic output that works perfectly for social media posts.

items: Used exclusively for SFW content generation
Excellent for varied poses, full-body shots, and lifestyle scenes
Strong at generating natural backgrounds and environments
Produces photorealistic output ideal for social media platforms
This is where you will generate the majority of your public content

right.content: Seedream 4.5 is used only for NSFW content generation. This is the tool you will use for paid subscription content on platforms like Fanvue. Do not use Seedream for your public social media content.

items: Used exclusively for NSFW content generation
Produces highly photorealistic output — natural skin, lighting, and features
Strong face consistency when using reference images properly
Best for detailed, intimate content behind paywalls
Keep this content separate from your public-facing social media

blocks.content: Two Routes to Creating Your Base Model

There are two approaches people use to generate their base model. We strongly recommend the Ethical Route — building your persona from scratch. The Shady Route is included here only so you are aware it exists and understand how others do it. We do not endorse or recommend it. Regardless of which route someone takes, the end goal is the same: two solid reference images (face and body) that you will use for every generation going forward.

left.content: We do not recommend this approach. Some operators use a real reference image from Pinterest or similar platforms as a starting point, then modify the appearance so the final result looks nothing like the original. This is included for awareness only — we advise against it.

items: Find a reference image on Pinterest of the look or aesthetic you want
Feed the reference image to ChatGPT or Gemini
Ask it to give you a perfect 1:1 description of the model from the waist up, standing in front of a white wall
Use that text description as your generation prompt — the white wall ensures no background items bleed into your final image
Generate your base model using this description

left.title: Do NOT Clone the Reference
left.content: We do not condone or recommend this method. Using someone's likeness as a base — even if modified — carries real legal and ethical risks. If you choose to go this route despite our recommendation, you must change the appearance so it looks nothing like the original. Do not create an exact clone — that is not allowed and can get you in serious trouble. The Ethical Route below is the recommended approach.

right.content: This approach builds your persona entirely from scratch using only text prompts — no reference images from real people involved at any stage.

items: Write a detailed text description of your ideal persona from scratch
Include specifics: face shape, hair color and style, skin tone, eye color, body type, age range
Generate 20-30 variations using your text prompt
Review all outputs and select the most natural, consistent result
Refine your prompt based on what works until you lock in the look

right.content: Prompt: 'Hyper-realistic, front-facing studio portrait of a woman with a modern goth aesthetic. Symmetrical composition, centered framing, eye-level shot. She has pale skin, sharp winged eyeliner, and matte black lipstick. Straight raven-black hair with micro-bangs. Wearing a simple black high-neck velvet outfit. Soft, even cinematic lighting to capture skin texture and fine details without heavy shadows. 8k resolution, shot on 85mm lens, neutral grey background, highly detailed features.'

You will need to play around with your prompt to get good results — do not expect perfection on the first try. Adjust wording, swap out details, and regenerate until the output matches what you are going for. Once you have a solid base image, run it through the Skin Enhancer on Higgsfield to really make the image look realistic. This step makes a noticeable difference in skin texture and overall believability.

blocks.content: Building Your Two Reference Images

Regardless of which route you chose, you now need to create your two core reference images. These two images are what you will use as reference inputs for every single generation from this point forward.

left.content: Your first reference image should be a clear, well-lit shot of your model's face from the waist up. This is your primary identity reference — every future generation will use this to maintain face consistency.

items: Waist-up shot with face clearly visible and facing the camera
Clean white or neutral background — no distracting objects
Natural lighting, natural expression
No artifacts, distortions, or extra fingers
Save at full resolution — this is your most important asset

right.content: Your second reference image should be a full-body picture of the model with her face turned towards the camera and her back visible as well. This gives you an accurate depiction of her body that you can reference for all future content.

items: Full-body shot showing front and proportions clearly
Face turned towards the camera so it stays consistent with Reference 1
Clean background — same white wall approach works best
Natural pose, no dramatic angles
Save at full resolution alongside your face reference

blocks.title: Critical Rule
blocks.content: You now have your two base reference images — the face and the body. These two images will be used as reference inputs for ALL of your generations going forward. Your base model is your brand. Every image you generate from this point must look like the same person. If you cannot recreate the face reliably, go back and generate a better seed image. Do not proceed until you are confident in both references.

sections.header: Lesson 3: Face Consistency & Building Your Content Library

blocks.content: Consistency is what separates a believable AI influencer from an obvious fake. Your audience will scroll through your page. If the face changes between posts, trust is destroyed instantly. This lesson covers how to maintain your model's look using Nano Banana 2 for all SFW content, why you need to generate multiple versions of every image, and how to build a content library before you launch.

blocks.title: Inconsistent Faces Will Get You Caught
blocks.content: If your model's face looks different between posts, people will notice. Followers will get suspicious, call you out in comments, or report your page. One inconsistent image is all it takes to lose credibility. Every single image you post must look like the same person — no exceptions. This is the number one reason AI pages get exposed.
The Reference Image Method

Nano Banana 2 is your main tool for all SFW content generation. It supports reference image workflows — you upload your seed images from Lesson 2 as references, and the tool uses them to maintain your character's face and body across new generations. This is how you keep every image looking like the same person.

items: Start with your two locked-in reference images from Lesson 2 (face + body)
Upload them as reference inputs when generating new images in Nano Banana 2
Write a new prompt describing the desired scene, outfit, or setting
The tool preserves the face and body from your references while creating the new scene
Generate at least 3 versions of every image — pick the best one
Only keep images where the face matches your reference consistently
Always use both your face and body reference images together
Higher resolution reference images produce better consistency
Never change your primary reference images once your page is live
Adjust reference strength settings if the face drifts — higher strength means more consistency but less variety

right.content: Why You Must Generate at Least 3 Per Image

Nano Banana 2 will not produce a perfect result every time. Some generations will have inconsistent faces, unrealistic features, or subtle artifacts. This is normal — it is part of the process. You need to generate at least 3 versions of every piece of content and pick the best one.

items: Generation 1 might have a slightly different jawline or eye shape
Generation 2 might nail the face but have a weird hand or background
Generation 3 might be the one that looks perfect
Sometimes you will need 5-10 generations to get one usable image
Never settle for 'close enough' — if the face is off, regenerate

right.title: 3 Generations Minimum
right.content: Make this your rule: never post the first image you generate. Always generate at least 3 versions, compare them against your reference images, and only keep the one that matches best. This single habit will save your page from getting exposed.

blocks.title: Visual Consistency Check
blocks.content: Before posting any image, place it next to your last 3 posts. If the face does not look like the same person at a glance, do not post it. Regenerate until it matches. This 5-second test saves you from destroying trust with your audience.
Building Your Content Library

You should never be scrambling for content. Before you post anything publicly, build a library of at least 30-50 images. This gives you a buffer and lets you plan your content calendar without pressure. Running out of content mid-warmup kills momentum and consistency.
Batch Generation Strategy

Dedicate specific sessions to content generation rather than creating images one at a time. A focused session with Nano Banana 2 should produce 10-20 usable SFW images. Remember — generate at least 3 versions of each and only keep the best.

items: Set aside 2-3 dedicated sessions per week for content generation
Generate 10-20 usable images per session with varied prompts
Use Nano Banana 2 for all SFW content (Instagram, Threads, TikTok)
Change outfits, settings, and poses across the batch
Sort images into categories immediately after generation
Keep a 'rejected' folder — you may salvage these later with editing
Target: build a 2-week content buffer before launching any account

blocks.title: Minimum Before Launch
blocks.content: Do NOT launch any social media account until you have at least 30 quality images saved and sorted across multiple categories. Running out of content mid-warmup kills momentum and consistency. Your content library is your business inventory — stock it before you open the store.

summary_points: Choose your niche based on market demand, not personal preference — girl-next-door and fitness niches have the highest proven demand
Seedream 4.5 and Nano Banana 2 are your primary image generation tools — master both for different content types
Your base model's face is your brand — generate 20-30 variations and select the strongest as your seed image
Use the reference image method in Nano Banana 2 to maintain face consistency across all future SFW generations
Always generate at least 3 versions of every image — Nano Banana 2 will produce inconsistent or unrealistic results, so pick only the best
Lock in face structure, body type, skin tone, and eye color from day one — only outfits, lighting, and settings should vary
Build a library of 30-50 images sorted into categories before launching any account
